skill.name: statsmodels
skill.description: Guidance for using statsmodels to fit statistical models, run diagnostics, compare inference results, and forecast time series in Python.
skill.summary: Use statsmodels for regression, GLM, discrete choice, diagnostics, and time series analysis.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium patterns, but review found they are documentation false positives in Markdown examples. The files provide statsmodels guidance and code snippets, with no prompt injection, no active command execution wrapper, and no data exfiltration logic. The only retained risk factor is benign hardcoded links to official statsmodels documentation.

low_findings.title: Benign Hardcoded Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The skill lists official statsmodels documentation URLs for user reference. These are static Markdown links, not code that sends data or makes network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are plain Markdown links under Getting Help. No fetch, curl, telemetry, credential handling, or automatic request logic is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Script Detections Are Python Import Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Dynamic import and function-constructor detections map to ordinary Python import statements and plotting title calls in Markdown examples. They are not executable JavaScript and do not create dynamic code execution in the skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows statistical Python snippets such as sklearn imports and Matplotlib title text. There is no eval, Function constructor, or runtime script loader.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Command Detections Are Markdown Fences And Search Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Ruby or shell backtick findings are caused by Markdown code fences and a short bash search example. The skill does not define a tool, script, or command runner that executes user input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is enclosed in Markdown examples for Python or documentation search. No subprocess, shell expansion, or executable entry point was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Malware Keyword Detections Are Statistical Terms
dangerous_patterns.description: Weak crypto, C2, and reconnaissance detections are substring matches in statistical terminology such as AIC/BIC, HC2, chi-squared, residuals, and model formulas. No malware intent, persistence, system discovery, or cryptographic operation is present.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are ordinary statistical notation or variable names inside modeling examples. I found no code for cryptography, command and control, or host reconnaissance.

content.value_statement: Statistical modeling often requires the right model, diagnostics, and interpretation choices. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through statsmodels workflows for rigorous Python analysis.
actual_capabilities: Guides OLS, WLS, GLS, quantile regression, and robust standard error workflows.
Explains GLM choices for binomial, Poisson, Gamma, Negative Binomial, and related families.
Covers binary, multinomial, count, zero-inflated, and ordinal outcome models.
Provides time series guidance for ARIMA, SARIMAX, VAR, state space models, and forecasting.
Supports residual diagnostics, influence checks, heteroskedasticity tests, and model comparison.
Helps interpret coefficients, odds ratios, rate ratios, marginal effects, and confidence intervals.
limitations: Requires the user to provide data context and analysis goals.
Does not run models unless the host environment has Python dependencies installed.
Does not replace statistical judgment for study design or causal identification.
Examples are guidance and may need adaptation for specific datasets.

use_cases.title: Regression Analysis For Researchers
use_cases.description: Fit linear models, check assumptions, compare specifications, and explain coefficient estimates.

use_cases.title: Operational Forecasting For Analysts
use_cases.description: Select time series models, test stationarity, produce forecasts, and evaluate forecast accuracy.

use_cases.title: Econometric Modeling For Data Scientists
use_cases.description: Use discrete choice, count, and GLM methods for inference-focused modeling in Python.

prompt_templates.title: Choose A Model
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a dataset with [outcome type] and predictors [predictors]. Recommend a statsmodels model and explain why.

prompt_templates.title: Check Regression Assumptions
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my OLS workflow for [analysis goal]. Suggest statsmodels diagnostics for residuals, heteroskedasticity, influence, and multicollinearity.

prompt_templates.title: Interpret GLM Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Help interpret my GLM results for [family and link]. Explain coefficients, transformed effects, uncertainty, and model checks.

prompt_templates.title: Design A Forecasting Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a statsmodels workflow for forecasting [series] with [frequency]. Include stationarity tests, model selection, diagnostics, and validation.

output_examples.input: User asks how to analyze a binary outcome with several predictors.
output: Recommendation to use Logit or Probit instead of OLS.
Steps to add an intercept, fit the model, review convergence, and compute marginal effects.
Guidance for reporting odds ratios, predicted probabilities, AUC, and diagnostic checks.

output_examples.input: User asks why a Poisson model fits count data poorly.
output: Explanation of overdispersion and excess zeros as common causes.
Suggested checks using dispersion statistics and residual plots.
Recommendation to compare Negative Binomial or zero-inflated models when appropriate.

output_examples.input: User needs a monthly sales forecast with uncertainty intervals.
output: Workflow for plotting the series, testing stationarity, and checking seasonality.
Model selection guidance for ARIMA or SARIMAX with validation on a holdout period.
Reporting plan for forecast intervals, residual diagnostics, and forecast error metrics.

best_practices: State the outcome type, sampling design, and analysis goal before choosing a model.
Run diagnostics and sensitivity checks before interpreting statistical significance.
Report assumptions, transformations, uncertainty intervals, and model comparison criteria.
anti_patterns: Do not use OLS for binary, count, or ordinal outcomes when a specific model is available.
Do not interpret transformed-link coefficients without converting them to an appropriate scale.
Do not trust time series forecasts without stationarity checks, residual diagnostics, and validation.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps choose, fit, diagnose, and interpret statsmodels statistical models in Python.

faq.question: Can it help with linear regression?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers OLS, WLS, GLS, robust standard errors, diagnostics, and model comparison.

faq.question: Does it support logistic regression?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes binary outcome guidance, odds ratios, predicted probabilities, and marginal effects.

faq.question: Can it guide time series forecasting?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers stationarity tests, ARIMA, SARIMAX, VAR, forecast intervals, and validation.

faq.question: Does it execute analysis automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Execution depends on the host environment and installed packages.

faq.question: Is this suitable for causal claims?
faq.answer: It can support causal modeling workflows, but users must justify design assumptions and identification strategy.
